title: Vital Signs, Physical Examination & Palpation Quiz

questions.text: A patient with bluish discoloration (cyanosis) most likely has:
Erythema (redness) is most commonly associated with:
An ashen (grey, dusky) appearance suggests:
Jaundice (yellow discoloration) is most commonly caused by:
The normal ORAL temperature range is:
The normal RECTAL/EAR temperature range is:
A fever that varies less than a degree within 24 hours is called:
A fever elevation with wide variations is best described as:
A fever that elevates but returns to normal and subnormal levels is:
The most common cause of fever is:
A pulse of 110 bpm in an adult is best described as:
The normal systolic pressure range is:
The normal diastolic pressure range is:
Systolic pressure reflects which event?

answers.text: Peak force during contraction of the left ventricle

questions.text: Hypotension typically indicates:
Hypertension is associated with:
Which is a correct way to count a respiratory rate?
The normal resting adult respiratory rate is:
Eupnea is defined as:

answers.text: Normal respiratory rate, rhythm, and depth

questions.text: A respiratory rate of 24 bpm in an adult is best described as:
Common causes of bradypnea include:
Which pattern is the complete absence of breathing?
Hyperpnea is best defined as:

answers.text: Labored, rapid, high-volume breathing caused by metabolic acidosis
Increased rate and depth of breathing, typically caused by metabolic disorders
Breathing that is difficult unless lying flat
Completely irregular breathing with apneic pauses

questions.text: Kussmaul's breathing is most commonly seen with:

answers.text: Late stages of severe metabolic acidosis

questions.text: Cheyne-Stokes respirations are best described as:

answers.text: Prolonged inspiration followed by short expiration
Irregular breathing with long apneic pauses but equal depth
Gradually increasing then decreasing in depth and rate with periods of apnea
Labored breathing with increased rate and volume

questions.text: All of the following are listed causes of Cheyne-Stokes respirations EXCEPT:
Cheyne-Stokes breathing can occur as a normal side effect of which medication?
Biot's respirations are characterized by:

answers.text: Gradually increasing and decreasing depth with apnea
Irregular breathing with long periods of apnea; each breath has the same depth
Steady deep breaths at a rapid rate

questions.text: A cause of Biot's respirations is:

answers.text: Increased intracranial pressure or damage to the medulla oblongata

questions.text: Ataxic breathing most commonly indicates:

answers.text: Damage to the medulla oblongata and a very poor prognosis

questions.text: As ataxic breathing deteriorates, it begins to merge with:
Apneustic breathing is caused by damage to the:
Apneustic breathing is characterized by:

answers.text: Short rapid inspirations and long expirations
Prolonged gasping inspiration followed by extremely short, insufficient expiration

questions.text: Orthopnea is most strongly associated with:
Difficulty breathing unless lying flat, relieved by a recumbent position, is:
Trepopnea is:

answers.text: Difficulty breathing on one side but not when the patient turns to the other side
Sudden nocturnal dyspnea relieved by sitting up

questions.text: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea (PND) is caused by:

answers.text: Gradual transfer of fluid in the lower extremities to the lungs when lying flat

questions.text: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea is relieved when the patient:
Pursed-lip breathing is most commonly a finding in:
Which of the following does PERRLA stand for?

answers.text: Pupils Equal, Round, Reactive to Light and Accommodation
Pulse, Effort, Rhythm, Rate, Level of Arousal
Perfusion, Edema, Respirations, Response, Level, Alertness
Pressure, Elevation, Redness, Range, Lines, Anatomy

questions.text: Normal pupillary reflexes require which cranial nerves to be intact?
Inspection of the neck helps identify all of the following EXCEPT:
In a normal chest, the anteroposterior (A-P) diameter is:
An increased A-P diameter (barrel chest) is a classic sign of:
Pectus excavatum is defined as:

answers.text: Depression of part or all of the sternum

questions.text: Pectus carinatum is defined as:
Scoliosis is a:

answers.text: Combination of A-P and lateral curvature

questions.text: Kyphosis is a:

answers.text: Increased A-P diameter of the chest

questions.text: Kyphoscoliosis is:

answers.text: A combination of kyphosis and scoliosis

questions.text: Digital clubbing is caused by:
Digital clubbing is present when:

answers.text: The angle of the nail bed and skin increases

questions.text: Palpation is best defined as:

answers.text: Listening for internal sounds with a stethoscope
Use of the hands to feel for body movement, lumps, masses, and skin characteristics
Tapping the chest to elicit sound

questions.text: During chest palpation, you assess all of the following EXCEPT:

answers.text: Breath sounds over the lung fields

questions.text: Vocal fremitus is:

answers.text: Vibrations created by the vocal cords transmitted down the tracheobronchial tree to the chest wall
The sound of airflow through collapsed alveoli
A crackling sensation from subcutaneous air
The feeling of a pulse against the chest wall

questions.text: Tactile fremitus is:

answers.text: The act of measuring BP by touch alone
When vibrations are felt on the chest wall as the patient talks
Chest wall pain with deep inspiration

questions.text: A pulse that is difficult to depress with the fingertips is best described as:
A pulse described as 'beat beat pause · beat beat pause' in a continuous pattern is:
A pulse with no discernible pattern at all is:
Pulsus paradoxus is defined as:

answers.text: A systolic pressure drop of more than 10 mmHg during inhalation at rest
A diastolic rise of more than 20 mmHg with exhalation
A pulse that disappears with deep breathing
An abnormally fast pulse with fever

questions.text: Pulsus paradoxus is caused by all of the following EXCEPT:
When palpating a pulse, which fingers should be used?

answers.text: Pads of the index and middle fingers

questions.text: The most commonly used site for pulse palpation is the:
The posterior tibial pulse is located:

answers.text: Groove between the first and second toes
Posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus

questions.text: The dorsalis pedis pulse is found in the:

answers.text: Groove between the first and second toes, slightly medial on the dorsum of the foot
Lateral ankle behind the lateral malleolus

questions.text: The popliteal pulse is located:
The femoral pulse is palpated:

answers.text: Between the pubic symphysis and anterior superior iliac spine

questions.text: The temporal pulse is located:

answers.text: Temple, directly in front of the ear

questions.text: To palpate the trachea for deviation, you should:

answers.text: Press the thumb against the thyroid cartilage
Depress the index finger into the suprasternal notch

questions.text: Before taking a blood pressure, the patient should be seated for at least:
Proper positioning for a blood pressure measurement includes:

answers.text: Arm hanging freely below heart level
Arm resting on a table with the midpoint of the upper arm at heart level
Patient standing with arm at the side

questions.text: During a blood pressure measurement, the patient's legs should be:
The bladder in a BP cuff should encircle what portion of the arm?
Using a BP cuff that is either too large or too small most commonly results in:
The BP cuff should be placed:

answers.text: Mid-way between the shoulder and the elbow, with markings centered over the brachial artery

questions.text: When taking a BP, the cuff should first be inflated to what pressure before continuing until the pulse is no longer felt?
While auscultating a BP, the FIRST sound heard represents the:
The diastolic value is noted when:

answers.text: The last sound or change in sound is heard

questions.text: If a BP reading is uncertain, how long should you wait before reattempting?

answers.text: At least 10 seconds with the cuff still partially inflated
At least 30 seconds with the cuff fully deflated
At least 5 minutes with the cuff still inflated

questions.text: A BP cuff should NOT remain inflated for longer than:
